pattern: Conﬂ uent white matter lesions in a child

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is bilateral, symmetric, confluent regions of increased signal intensity within the subcortical and periventricular white matter on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences. There is a striking posterior predominance, classically involving the parieto-occipital white matter and the splenium of the corpus callosum.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include nodular or garland-like enhancement along the leading edges of the demyelinating lesions on post-gadolinium T1-weighted images, representing active inflammation. The visual, auditory, and corticospinal tracts may also be involved, often with associated volume loss in advanced stages.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of discrete, asymmetric ovoid plaques oriented perpendicular to the ventricles to suggest multiple sclerosis. Furthermore, there is no history or imaging feature of a self-limiting, patchy process with deep grey matter involvement typical of acute disseminated encephalomyelitis.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the striking posterior, perfectly symmetric, confluent white matter dysmyelination with leading-edge enhancement in a child.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Dysmyelinating disease (adrenoleukodystrophy), given the symmetric, confluent T2 hyperintensity with profound posterior predominance and splenium involvement. This X-linked recessive metabolic disorder leads to the accumulation of toxic metabolites and classically presents in young males with visual, auditory, or behavioural disturbances.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Acute disseminating encephalomyelitis (ADEM), however this typically presents as a monophasic illness following an antecedent viral infection or vaccination, with multifocal, less perfectly symmetric, and more patchy white matter lesions rather than a continuous confluent posterior spread. Additionally, ADEM lacks the classic advancing edge of enhancement seen in adrenoleukodystrophy.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Multiple sclerosis, given that missing this could delay appropriate disease-modifying therapies in a paediatric patient. However, multiple sclerosis classically presents with discrete, asymmetric perivenular plaques (Dawson fingers) and a relapsing-remitting course, which does not match this profoundly symmetric, confluent pattern.
differentials.unifyingSummary: Taken together, these findings point to a unifying diagnosis of a metabolic dysmyelinating disorder affecting the central nervous system and, classically, the adrenal glands.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the extent of white matter involvement and use advanced imaging techniques to differentiate this metabolic leukodystrophy from acquired demyelinating processes.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to evaluate the progression of the white matter changes and to determine if the condition is relentlessly progressive, as expected in a leukodystrophy, or resolving, as would be expected in ADEM.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend an MRI of the brain with MR spectroscopy, as this modality is superior here for confirming the diagnosis prior to advanced structural changes, typically showing reduced N-acetyl-aspartate and increased choline which are important for planning possible interventions like bone marrow transplantation.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences to map the extent of dysmyelination, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to identify the classic leading-edge enhancement indicative of active disease.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Paediatric Neurology and Genetics MDT. My report would clearly document the precise anatomical distribution of the white matter involvement, the presence of active enhancing edges, and the spectroscopy findings to expedite biochemical assays and consider urgent bone marrow transplantation.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is bilateral, symmetric, confluent regions of increased signal intensity within the subcortical and periventricular white matter on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences. There is a striking posterior predominance, classically involving the parieto-occipital white matter and the splenium of the corpus callosum. Supporting features include nodular or garland-like enhancement along the leading edges of the demyelinating lesions on post-gadolinium T1-weighted images, representing active inflammation. The visual, auditory, and corticospinal tracts may also be involved, often with associated volume loss in advanced stages. Importantly, there is no evidence of discrete, asymmetric ovoid plaques oriented perpendicular to the ventricles to suggest multiple sclerosis. Furthermore, there is no history or imaging feature of a self-limiting, patchy process with deep grey matter involvement typical of acute disseminated encephalomyelitis. The feature that most influences my thinking is the striking posterior, perfectly symmetric, confluent white matter dysmyelination with leading-edge enhancement in a child.

The appearances are most in keeping with Dysmyelinating disease (adrenoleukodystrophy), given the symmetric, confluent T2 hyperintensity with profound posterior predominance and splenium involvement. This X-linked recessive metabolic disorder leads to the accumulation of toxic metabolites and classically presents in young males with visual, auditory, or behavioural disturbances. The principle differential would be Acute disseminating encephalomyelitis (ADEM), however this typically presents as a monophasic illness following an antecedent viral infection or vaccination, with multifocal, less perfectly symmetric, and more patchy white matter lesions rather than a continuous confluent posterior spread. Additionally, ADEM lacks the classic advancing edge of enhancement seen in adrenoleukodystrophy. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Multiple sclerosis, given that missing this could delay appropriate disease-modifying therapies in a paediatric patient. However, multiple sclerosis classically presents with discrete, asymmetric perivenular plaques (Dawson fingers) and a relapsing-remitting course, which does not match this profoundly symmetric, confluent pattern. Taken together, these findings point to a unifying diagnosis of a metabolic dysmyelinating disorder affecting the central nervous system and, classically, the adrenal glands.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the extent of white matter involvement and use advanced imaging techniques to differentiate this metabolic leukodystrophy from acquired demyelinating processes. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to evaluate the progression of the white matter changes and to determine if the condition is relentlessly progressive, as expected in a leukodystrophy, or resolving, as would be expected in ADEM. I would recommend an MRI of the brain with MR spectroscopy, as this modality is superior here for confirming the diagnosis prior to advanced structural changes, typically showing reduced N-acetyl-aspartate and increased choline which are important for planning possible interventions like bone marrow transplantation. I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences to map the extent of dysmyelination, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to identify the classic leading-edge enhancement indicative of active disease. This should go to the Paediatric Neurology and Genetics MDT. My report would clearly document the precise anatomical distribution of the white matter involvement, the presence of active enhancing edges, and the spectroscopy findings to expedite biochemical assays and consider urgent bone marrow transplantation.
